article_title: I finally tried Obsidian again and realized why so many people use Notion instead
core_thesis: The author argues that while Obsidian offers a superior writing experience due to its local-first, markdown-based approach, its heavy reliance on third-party plugins for core functionality (like kanban boards and databases) and its poor sync experience (requiring either a paid subscription or complex self-hosting) make it less practical than Notion for users who need an all-in-one, out-of-the-box solution for writing and project management.
detailed_summary: The article recounts the author's attempt to switch from Notion to Obsidian for their writing workflow, which heavily depends on kanban boards to track articles through stages like ideation, outlining, approval, and writing. The author found that Obsidian's core experience is barebones, requiring plugins for almost every feature. However, many popular plugins, including kanban plugins, are abandoned or poorly maintained, leading to compatibility issues with newer Obsidian versions. The author also struggled with syncing: Obsidian's local-first design means syncing via iCloud or Google Drive is not instant, and the official Obsidian Sync service costs a monthly fee. The open-source Obsidian LiveSync plugin offers a self-hosted alternative but is complex to set up and maintain, even for someone with Docker and Linux experience. Ultimately, the author concludes that Notion's built-in databases, views, and instant cloud sync make it more capable and less time-consuming to use, despite preferring Obsidian's writing experience.

entities.name: Obsidian
entities.role: A markdown-based note-taking and writing application with a plugin ecosystem, developed by a small team.

entities.name: Notion
entities.role: A cloud-based all-in-one workspace for notes, databases, and project management, with built-in kanban boards and instant sync.

entities.name: Patrick Campanale
entities.role: Author of the article and a tech journalist who uses kanban boards for writing workflow.

entities.name: Obsidian LiveSync
entities.role: An open-source plugin for self-hosting instant sync for Obsidian, but complex to set up.

entities.name: Obsidian Git
entities.role: A plugin that syncs Obsidian vaults via Git, but is third-party and could be abandoned.

key_data_points: Obsidian has a small team of less than a dozen people, which limits native feature development and forces reliance on third-party plugins (🟢 relevant to your Red Teaming: plugin dependency creates a large attack surface for supply chain vulnerabilities).

Many Obsidian plugins become abandonware because they are maintained by single individuals who may lose interest or time; the author found multiple kanban plugins that were archived or looking for new maintainers (🟢 relevant to your Red Teaming: abandoned plugins may have unpatched security flaws).

Obsidian's official sync service costs a monthly fee, while free alternatives like iCloud/Google Drive are not instant; self-hosting via Obsidian LiveSync requires Docker and Linux server maintenance (🟢 relevant to your Homelab/DevOps: self-hosting sync is a practical homelab project but demands ongoing effort).

Notion has 100 million users compared to Obsidian's 1 million, indicating a much larger user base and likely more stable feature support (🟢 relevant to your student needs: Notion's popularity means more community resources and templates for productivity).

The author spends more time configuring Obsidian (finding plugins, setting up sync) than actually writing, which defeats the purpose of a writing tool (🟢 relevant to your productivity: time spent on tooling should not outweigh actual work output).
conclusions: Obsidian's plugin-dependent architecture leads to maintenance and compatibility issues, making it unreliable for workflows that depend on specific features like kanban boards. Users should evaluate whether the core writing experience outweighs the cost of plugin management.

Notion's built-in databases, views, and instant sync provide a more seamless experience for users who need an all-in-one solution, despite its block-based editor being less preferred by the author. The trade-off between flexibility and convenience is a key factor in choosing a note-taking tool.

For users with homelab skills, self-hosting Obsidian sync via LiveSync is possible but requires significant setup and maintenance, which may not be worth the effort for those who prioritize simplicity. The author's experience suggests that unless you are willing to invest time in configuration, Notion is the more practical choice.
personal_relevance_summary: This article is relevant to you as a student and programmer because it compares two popular productivity tools (Obsidian and Notion) that are widely used for note-taking, project management, and writing. As someone interested in homelabbing and DevOps, the discussion of self-hosting Obsidian LiveSync with Docker provides a practical example of a homelab project, though the author warns it's complex. For your Red Teaming interests, the article highlights the security risks of relying on third-party plugins that may be abandoned or have vulnerabilities. As a student, the productivity insights—such as the trade-off between tool configuration and actual work—can help you choose a tool that minimizes friction and maximizes output. The article also reinforces the importance of evaluating whether a tool's ecosystem is sustainable for your long-term needs.
extra_advice: Consider using Obsidian for pure writing and a separate, dedicated kanban tool (like Trello or a self-hosted Kanboard) to avoid plugin dependency. This hybrid approach gives you the best writing experience without sacrificing reliable project management. If you do choose Obsidian, regularly audit your plugins for abandonment and have a migration plan to avoid workflow disruptions.
